issues.description: Says G0 is where 'most of your neurons and mature muscle cells live ... alive and working, just not dividing.' This makes G0 sound like a permanent dead-end. Some G0 cells (liver hepatocytes, lymphocytes) can re-enter the cycle on the right signal. A student memorizing this could be tripped by an FRQ that asks 'can a G0 cell ever divide again?'

'In G1 ... the DNA is still one copy per chromosome' is the exact phrase that produces the most common AP misconception: students conclude G1 cells are haploid. The script never reassures the student that a G1 somatic cell is still 2n (diploid) — it only has one chromatid per chromosome. The number of chromosomes does not change across S phase; the amount of DNA doubles. This distinction is testable and the script misses it.

Prophase description says 'the spindle starts forming from the centrosomes' — but earlier (section 05) the script told the student centrosomes are built in G2. A careful student will ask: are the centrosomes that nucleate the spindle the SAME ones built in G2? Yes — but the script never connects those two facts. Bridging sentence needed.

Lists three checkpoints but never says the outcome of failing each one. G1 fail → G0 is mentioned, but G2 fail and M-spindle fail outcomes are not stated. A student will assume 'apoptosis' for both, which is partly right but the M-spindle checkpoint typically *delays* anaphase via the mitotic checkpoint complex rather than triggering immediate apoptosis. Mismatch invites a wrong FRQ answer.

The 'one of two things' framing for p53 (pause-and-repair OR apoptosis) is clean but hides the third real outcome: p53 can also drive senescence (permanent arrest without death). Not required by CED but a strong AP student reading further will encounter this and feel the lesson was incomplete.

'Cancer almost always requires multiple mutations stacking up' is true but vague. The student is not told why — no mention that oncogene activation alone is usually blocked by tumor suppressors, which is *why* multi-hit is required. The mechanistic link between the two gene categories is the actual insight, and it's left implicit.

The 'gas pedal stuck down vs. brakes cut' analogy is solid but the script gives the AP exam tell ('one mutation' = oncogene, 'both alleles' = tumor suppressor) as a pattern-match shortcut without warning the student that real exam questions sometimes describe a dominant-negative tumor suppressor or a haploinsufficient case. A student who memorizes the shortcut as a law will get the trick question wrong.

summary: The lesson teaches well but leaves three load-bearing nuances unsaid: ploidy stays 2n across S phase, G0 can be reversible, and the multi-hit cancer model has a mechanistic reason. A motivated student will build correct intuitions everywhere else, but these gaps are where AP exam writers love to live.
